coredata.dc:title: Electrospun nanofibers of polycaprolactone loaded with chitosan/carbon quantum dots composite for adsorptive removal of Cd(II) ions from wastewater: Adsorption, kinetics, thermodynamics, and BOX Behnken design

coredata.dc:description: The research focused on assessing the improvements in stability and effectiveness regarding the elimination of Cd(II) ions. Specifically, this study involved the development of a novel electrospun nanofibrous membrane composed of chitosan (CS), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and polycaprolactone (PCL). The membrane's primary application is the elimination of Cd(II) ions, from aqueous solutions. By precisely fine-tuning the electrospinning process parameters, we succeeded in optimizing the membrane. The characterizations performed using FTIR, XRD, XPS, SEM and EDX confirm the successful formation of a robustly crosslinked CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane. This thorough investigation not only showcases the material's textural characteristics but also underscores its potential for various applications. The examination further analyzed the influence of numerous factors, such as dosage, pH, temperature, and initial concentration, by the adsorption mechanism. The research incorporated equilibrium assessments in conjunction with kinetic evaluations to evaluate the characteristics of adsorption. The results showed that the Langmuir isotherm model well captured the adsorption process and that it complied with pseudo-second-order kinetics values. The fact that metal adsorption rises with temperature indicates that endothermic reactions and spontaneous behavior are both features of the adsorption process. The data suggests that the most effective situations for the elimination of Cd(II) ions in water purification are characterized by a pH level of 6, a amount of 0.02 g of CS/CQDs/PCL per 25 mL, and an adsorption capability measured at 340.05 mg/g precisely for the Cd(II) ions solution. In order to enhance the efficiency of the adsorbent in Cd(II) ions elimination from water, it is crucial to consider multiple significant factors. Systematic trials using the Box-Behnken design and response surface methods, made possible by the Design-Expert software, demonstrated significant improvements in the adsorption efficiency. A thorough assessment of the adsorbent's reusability conducted over five consecutive adsorption and desorption cycles shows a high degree of consistency in its removal efficacy.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The research focused on assessing the improvements in stability and effectiveness regarding the elimination of Cd(II) ions. Specifically, this study involved the development of a novel electrospun nanofibrous membrane composed of chitosan (CS), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and polycaprolactone (PCL). The membrane's primary application is the elimination of Cd(II) ions, from aqueous solutions. By precisely fine-tuning the electrospinning process parameters, we succeeded in optimizing the membrane. The characterizations performed using FTIR, XRD, XPS, SEM and EDX confirm the successful formation of a robustly crosslinked CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane. This thorough investigation not only showcases the material's textural characteristics but also underscores its potential for various applications. The examination further analyzed the influence of numerous factors, such as dosage, pH, temperature, and initial concentration, by the adsorption mechanism. The research incorporated equilibrium assessments in conjunction with kinetic evaluations to evaluate the characteristics of adsorption. The results showed that the Langmuir isotherm model well captured the adsorption process and that it complied with pseudo-second-order kinetics values. The fact that metal adsorption rises with temperature indicates that endothermic reactions and spontaneous behavior are both features of the adsorption process. The data suggests that the most effective situations for the elimination of Cd(II) ions in water purification are characterized by a pH level of 6, a amount of 0.02 g of CS/CQDs/PCL per 25 mL, and an adsorption capability measured at 340.05 mg/g precisely for the Cd(II) ions solution. In order to enhance the efficiency of the adsorbent in Cd(II) ions elimination from water, it is crucial to consider multiple significant factors. Systematic trials using the Box-Behnken design and response surface methods, made possible by the Design-Expert software, demonstrated significant improvements in the adsorption efficiency. A thorough assessment of the adsorbent's reusability conducted over five consecutive adsorption and desorption cycles shows a high degree of consistency in its removal efficacy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The elimination of heavy metal ions from wastewater holds considerable significance due to various factors that critically influence environmental health, public safety, and adherence to regulatory standards. Elements such as lead, mercury, cadmium, and arsenic exhibit toxic properties that can inflict serious damage on aquatic ecosystems upon release into water bodies. Therefore, their removal is imperative to safeguard biodiversity and ensure the stability and functionality of ecological systems [1]. Moreover, the presence of these pollutants in contaminated water significantly threatens human health, contributing to neurological disorders, renal impairment, and different types of cancer. This underscores the importance of robust wastewater treatment systems to protect public health, especially for populations dependent on water sources that may be tainted. Numerous nations enforce rigorous regulations regarding the permissible levels of metal ions in wastewater outputs, necessitating compliance from industries to prevent legal ramifications and maintain sustainable practices [2]. The presence of heavy metals can significantly inhibit microbial processes in biological wastewater treatment systems, which can reduce their overall effectiveness. Removing these metals is crucial to prevent contamination of soil and groundwater, as they have the potential to leach into these ecosystems, causing long-term environmental harm [3]. This issue is particularly critical for safeguarding agricultural land from the accumulation of heavy metals, which can negatively impact sectors such as agriculture, fisheries, and tourism, leading to potential economic repercussions [4]. The efficient removal of heavy metals is in line with sustainable development objectives that focus on providing clean water and sanitation, thereby supporting environmental sustainability and improving community well-being. In summary, implementing effective strategies to address heavy metal contamination is vital for environmental protection, public health, and the promotion of sustainable development [5]. The World Health Organization has established a advice stipulating that the allowable concentration of cadmium (II) in wastewater designated for agricultural applications must not surpass 0.003 ppm (ppm). This rigorous threshold is established in light of cadmium's considerable toxicity and its propensity to bioaccumulate within the food chain, which presents notable health hazards for both humans and animals. Consequently, it becomes imperative to devise and implement effective treatment strategies aimed at diminishing Cd(II) levels in wastewater, thereby aligning with these safety regulations and safeguarding public health [6].

The occurrence of cadmium ions in wastewater presents considerable toxicity threats to human health and environmental integrity, given that cadmium is classified as a heavy metal capable of inducing harm even at minimal concentrations [7]. Human exposure routes include the consumption of contaminated water, inhalation of particulate matter, or dermal contact [8]. Acute health effects may manifest as symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, and abdominal discomfort. In contrast, prolonged exposure is linked to serious health complications, including renal impairment, bone demineralization, and an elevated likelihood of malignancies, especially affecting the lungs, prostate, and kidneys [9]. Cadmium is recognized as a highly toxic substance in aquatic environments, posing significant risks to marine organisms such as fish, invertebrates, and microorganisms. Its toxicity can lead to adverse effects on physiological functions, resulting in diminished growth, reproductive success, and survival rates among these species [10,11]. Additionally, cadmium has the potential to bioaccumulate within the food chain, which endangers higher trophic level consumers, including humans who ingest affected fish and shellfish [12,13]. The metal can also migrate from contaminated water into soil, creating lasting soil contamination that negatively impacts plant growth and agricultural productivity [14,15]. The ecotoxicological implications of cadmium exposure are considerable, as it disrupts enzymatic activities, interferes with cellular functions, and heightens oxidative stress, ultimately weakening the immune responses of affected organisms. Due to these substantial health and environmental dangers, cadmium is regulated with stringent limits on wastewater discharges. Therefore, efficient management and removal strategies are crucial for reducing the associated risks and safeguarding both public health and environmental integrity [16].

Diverse approaches are implemented for the elimination of cadmium ions from water, each designed for particular conditions and levels of cadmium ions concentration [12]. One prevalent method is adsorption, which employs a range of adsorbents, including activated carbon, biosorbents such as agricultural byproducts like rice husks and chitosan and an array of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs). This technique proves to be particularly advantageous for handling low cadmium concentrations, as it allows for the optimization of removal efficiency through the alteration of the adsorbents' surface characteristics [17]. Chemical precipitation represents a widely utilized method for cadmium removal, wherein specific chemical reagents are added to generate insoluble cadmium compounds, or precipitates, that can be efficiently isolated via sedimentation or filtration techniques. The reagents employed, including sulfides, hydroxides, and carbonates, render this approach particularly adept at addressing environments with elevated cadmium concentrations. Conversely, ion exchange processes involve the use of specialized resin materials that facilitate the selective extraction of cadmium ions from a solution, substituting them with less toxic ions like sodium or potassium. This technique proves to be especially effective in scenarios where cadmium is present in low concentrations [18]. Membrane filtration methods including microfiltration, ultrafiltration, and reverse osmosis exploit the size and charge characteristics of cadmium ions to separate them and create the best possible effluent. However, the operational effectiveness of these techniques is contingent upon consistent maintenance and cleaning [19]. In contrast, electrochemical treatment strategies, like electrochemical reduction and electrocoagulation, utilize an electric current to enhance the extraction of cadmium ions, which frequently results in successful treatment efficacy. Bioremediation utilizes living organisms, particularly specific bacteria and plants, to remove cadmium from wastewater, a technique called phytoremediation [14]. Microorganisms can accumulate heavy metals while plants absorb cadmium via their roots. In contrast, thermal treatment focuses on solid waste using high temperatures like incineration for cadmium-contaminated materials, not liquid wastewater. Advanced oxidation processes create reactive species for cadmium precipitation and removal, but they primarily target organic pollutants [20]. Coagulation and flocculation introduce coagulants to bind cadmium ions into larger flocs, aiding removal through sedimentation or filtration, with common coagulants such as aluminum sulfate and ferric chloride. The selection of a precise method is contingent upon the distinct attributes of the wastewater and the levels of cadmium present. This often requires the integration of multiple techniques to achieve efficient management and extraction of cadmium, thereby mitigating environmental pollution and protecting public health [21].

The application of electrospinning for the fabrication of adsorbent materials aimed at cadmium ions removal from wastewater offers several important advantages that contribute to the effectiveness and efficiency of the adsorption process [22]. In particular, this method makes it possible to create nanofibers with an incredibly high surface area-to-volume ratio. By giving cadmium ions more active sites to adhere to, this property significantly increases the material's adsorption capacity and, as a result, improves removal efficiency. Electrospinning provides a methodical approach to controlling the dimensions and structure of fibers, yielding uniform and uniquely defined nanofibers tailored for specific adsorption characteristics [23]. This technique also allows for the deliberate design of high porosity within the electrospun nanofibers, which significantly enhances the mass transfer of cadmium ions from polluted water to the adsorbent interface. Such enhancement contributes to improved kinetics in the adsorption process, resulting in accelerated removal rates [24]. The diversity of materials available for electrospinning facilitates the integration of different polymers and functional substances, foremost to the progress of composite adsorbents with superior belongings. For instance, the introduction of functional groups or metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) can decidedly enhance the adsorption capability and selectivity for cadmium ions. Additionally, the electrospinning technique permits the tailored modification and functionalization of fibers, allowing alterations in their chemical makeup and surface properties, which in turn can increase their binding affinity for cadmium. The electrospun fibers produced can be efficiently collected as mats or nonwovens, facilitating their handling and incorporation into current wastewater treatment frameworks. A significant aspect is that many of the polymers utilized are either biodegradable or sourced from renewable materials, thereby promoting the environmental sustainability of these adsorbents, which is essential in efforts to mitigate pollution. Furthermore, the capability to regenerate electrospun adsorbents for multiple applications contributes to decreased operational expenses and improved long-term efficacy. In summary, the distinctive structural properties and the customizable attributes of electrospun nanofibers position them as a particularly effective solution for addressing cadmium contamination in wastewater processes [25].

The request of the Box-Behnken design (BBD) to enhance adsorption outcomes offerings various significant benefits that donate to the refinement of the research methodology and increase the effectiveness of adsorption systems. Primarily, BBD is classified as a statistical response surface methodology that necessitates a reduced number of experimental trials conversely to full factorial designs, all the while facilitating the simultaneous investigation of multiple variables. This level of efficiency not only optimizes the utilization of time and resources but also leads to a significant decrease in the expenses related to experimental materials and labor. Additionally, the design demonstrates notable effectiveness in accommodating second-order polynomial models. Such models are adept at accurately representing the curvature present in response surfaces, thereby offering a comprehensive statistical portrayal of the correlations between input variables and their impacts on adsorption efficiency. Moreover, BBD enables the evaluation of interaction effects among various factors, providing insight into how modifications in one variable affect others. This capability is essential for the optimization of intricate adsorption processes. BBD helps identify the optimal circumstances for reaching the maximum adsorption efficiency by methodically adjusting the levels of input parameters, which enhances the treatment process's overall effectiveness. A systematic methodology reduces the discrepancies and biases inherent in experimental procedures, which is crucial for obtaining dependable and repeatable outcomes necessary for validation purposes. Additionally, the analysis of data derived from BBD can be conducted effortlessly with a range of statistical software applications, generating accessible visual outputs like contour plots and surface plots. These visual representations aid in elucidating the impacts of various parameters and enhance the overall interpretative process. Regardless of the particular materials or contaminants present, the Box-Behnken design (BBD) can be applied to a diversity of adsorption systems because to its flexibility. This characteristic renders it a remarkably flexible instrument within adsorption research. In assumption, the Box-Behnken design provides a comprehensive and efficient framework for refining adsorption outcomes, therefore contributing significantly to the development of procedures and results in wastewater treatment and other applications [26].

This research presents an innovative electrospun nanofibrous membrane that integrates chitosan (CS), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and polycaprolactone (PCL), aimed at the effective extraction of Cd(II) ions. This advancement showcases notable improvements in chemical stability, adsorption capacity, functional group interactivity, cost efficiency, reliability, and overall efficacy. The inclusion of PCL serves a critical role as a structural framework that mitigates chitosan's natural solubility issues and mechanical vulnerability, thereby enhancing its chemical robustness in aqueous settings, especially in acidic environments. Furthermore, adding CQDs markedly elevates adsorption performance by providing a rich array of oxygen-containing functional groups (-OH, -COOH, CO). These groups work in concert with chitosan's amine (−NH2) and hydroxyl (-OH) moieties to facilitate improved chelation and targeted binding of Cd(II) ions. The electrospun nanofibrous structure significantly enhances surface area and porosity, thereby facilitating rapid and effective uptake of metal ions. This membrane is particularly noteworthy for its cost-effectiveness, as it utilizes biodegradable, renewable, and inexpensive materials, positioning it as a sustainable alternative to existing commercial adsorbents. Its capability for high reusability, achieved through straightforward acid or alkaline regeneration, guarantees its long-term usability in wastewater treatment processes. In contrast to traditional chitosan-based materials, which frequently experience instability under varying environmental conditions, this engineered membrane demonstrates reliable performance across a broad pH spectrum and is amenable to scaling for industrial use. These combined characteristics identify this hybrid nanofibrous membrane as an innovative solution for economical, high-efficiency, and sustainable remediation of Cd(II), effectively tackling significant challenges in heavy metal wastewater treatment while ensuring viability for large-scale applications.

This study presents several notable advantages, particularly in terms of chemical stability, which surpasses that of pure chitosan membranes. It also demonstrates enhanced adsorption efficiency attributable to the synergistic interactions between carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and chitosan (CS). The use of biodegradable and renewable materials contributes to the cost-effectiveness of the membrane. Moreover, its high surface area, selective adsorption characteristics, and ability to be reused position it as a viable option for large-scale wastewater treatment applications. Conversely, certain disadvantages are apparent, including the potential intricacies involved in the fabrication process, which demands meticulous regulation of electrospinning parameters to ensure consistent nanofiber morphology. Furthermore, the membrane's long-term stability when exposed to extreme environmental conditions—characterized by prolonged contact with highly acidic or alkaline wastewater—requires additional scrutiny. The scalability of CQDs production and its implications for cost-effectiveness in industrial contexts also merit further evaluation. In light of these drawbacks, the hybrid nanofibrous membrane developed within this study signifies a significant progression in the realm of heavy metal remediation, successfully balancing high performance, longevity, and sustainability.
The additional resources provide a comprehensive overview of the substances and tools utilized, as detailed in Tables S1 and S2.

The creation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was achieved through the combination of 30 mg of sugarcane bagasse with 70 mg of NaOH and 2400 mg of urea. This mixture underwent a freezing process for the overnight duration to yield a dissolved solution, which was subsequently subjected to ultrasonication for 5 min. Following this, the sonicated solution was processed in a domestic microwave at a power of 700 W for approximately 7 min [27].

Electrospinning represents an innovative method for the fabrication of nano- and micro-scale polymeric fibers tailored for specific applications, leveraging a wide variety of polymer materials. The underlying principle of electrospinning is grounded in the electro hydrodynamic process, which involves the electrification of a liquid droplet to create a charged jet. This jet undergoes a sequence of stretching and elongation, ultimately resulting in the creation of fibers. To clarify, the electrospinning process can be analyzed and categorized into four distinct stages: (i) The process of charging a liquid droplet leads to the subsequent development of a Taylor cone; (ii) Expansion of the electrically charged jet along a linear section; (iii) A reduction in jet thickness can be attributed to the influence of the electric field, which concurrently chiefs to the emergence of electrical bending instability, often referred to as whipping instability; and (iv) The process involves the solidification and aggregation of fibers.

Electrospinning was employed to create nanofiber scaffolds from mixing the two solutions of polycaprolactone (PCL) (15 % w/v, 90 % glacial acetic acid) and chitosan solution (2 % w/v, 90 % glacial acetic acid) with 70:30 v/v (PCL:CS). In order to encapsulate the CQDs into Nanofiber, a 5 % weight ratio of CS and PCL was added, mixed thoroughly [27]. A syringe pump running at a flow rate of 1 mL/h was used to transfer the solution of polymers into a glass syringe and administer it to a 20-gauge blunt-tip needle. The blunt-tip pointer was positioned 15 cm from the grounded collector plate, and a voltage between 27 and 28.5 kV was delivered using a high-voltage power source (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of Cd(II) and batch studies of the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane
ce:section.ce:para:
Before the adsorption assays, standard solutions containing 1000 mg/L of Cd(II) ions were prepared. In order to achieve the necessary Cd(II) ions concentrations, these original solution stocks were later diluted in the tests. All experiments were achieved at a measured temperature of 25 °C. A mixture of 0.02 g of CS/CQDs/PCL adsorbent and 25 mL of a 400 mg/L Cd(II) ions solution was made in order to evaluate the adsorption kinetics. At predetermined intervals, the Cd(II) solution's concentration variations were methodically documented. In a controlled experimental setup, 0.02 g of adsorbent was immersed in 25 mL of a Cd(II) solution, with concentrations varying from 22 to 442 mg/L, for 30 min [28,29]. This approach aimed to analyze the adsorption isotherms pertinent to the system. Additionally, for the evaluation of the adsorbents' reusability, the same quantity of adsorbent was placed in 25 mL of a 300 mg/L Cd(II) solution and maintained for a period of 100 min. To effectively remove the residual Cd(II), the adsorbent was subjected to centrifugation post-experimentation, and subsequently, their pH levels were modified to 6 through the addition of 0.01 M of hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide [30]. The adsorbent was subsequently immersed in EDTA solution for a duration of, after which they were collected through centrifugation. Afterwards, the gathered adsorbent underwent an hour-long drying process in an oven set at 60 °C. Five iterations of adsorption–desorption tests were showed to assess the performance of the created adsorbent. The effectiveness of the cycle adsorption procedure was measured using the original Cd(II) adsorption capability as a reference point. By carefully adding 0.1 mol/L of HCl or NaOH solutions dropwise, the Cd(II) solution's pH was systematically attuned to a range of 2 to 8. This adjustment aimed to investigate how varying the pH levels of the Cd(II) solution influences the adsorption capability of the selected adsorbents. A total of 0.02 g of CS/CQDs/PCL were immersed in 25 mL of a Cd(II) solutions with a concentration of 300 mg/L for a duration of 100 min. At specific time points, the Cd(II) ions concentration was assessed utilizing an atomic absorption spectrometer (AAS, Agilent AA-200). The efficacy of the adsorbent in sequestering materials is quantified through adsorption capability (qe, mg/g), which is articulated in Eq. (1). In parallel, the efficiency of the elimination mechanism was evaluated through Eq. (2), as elaborated below:

Response Surface Methodology (RSM) is utilized in scenarios characterized by multiple potential solutions to construct statistical models. The primary objective is to elucidate the relationship between the response variable and the various experimental conditions. Furthermore, this modeling method makes process improvements easier. RSM methodically conducts a number of organized experiments that take advantage of the process's inherent qualities in order to determine the best option within the operational framework as a whole [31,32]. Central Composite Design (CCD) is a frequently used method for process parameter optimization. With an emphasis on the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane, Eq. 1 measures the quantity of Cd(II) ions absorbed by gram of adsorbent during varied time intervals (t). Conversely, Eq. 2 serves to assess the efficacy of the elimination process [32,33]. The technique exhibited considerable dependence on three key variables: “contact time”, “adsorbent weight”, and “pH” of the solution. According to the data presented in Table S3, these limits were elected owing to their adverse influence on the adsorption capacity [34].

The Design Expert Software has facilitated the compilation of Table S3, which offers a inclusive impression of the highest and lowermost values associated with each limit. The table meticulously delineates the various mixtures of limits alongside their conforming results. Additionally, it encompasses a diverse range of experimental designs and configurations, incorporating 2n factorial runs, and P center runs, “2 × n” axial runs [35]. Eq. (3) serves as a tool for calculating the required number of experimental trials, a calculation that is contingent upon the quantity of input parameters.

In this study, “p” represents the overall count of required investigational trials, whereas “N” indicates the amount of process factors influencing the results. The variable “m” is specified as 3 within the context of this research [36]. The Central Composite design comprises three necessary phases: the identification of the model's factors, the establishment of the investigational framework, and the assessment of the model's predictive efficacy, all conducted with a thorough examination of the resulting data. Based on the findings of the previous studies, an empirical model has been developed to assess the function's performance with respect to different input variable combinations. This led to the creation of a quadratic regression model, as is shown in Eq. (4):
