In the context of this analysis, we use “I” to stand for the resistance constant and “j” for the speed constant. The symbols β0, βi, βii, and βij, respectively, are used to consistently represent the constants related to resistance, contact, speed, and the constant time. The efficacy of the planned polynomial classical equation was assessed using various statistical metrics, including R2, R2 Adj, and R2 Pred. A higher R2 value designates a more robust alignment among the model and the observed data, thereby suggesting an increased correctness in the model's predictive capabilities [37].

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns presented in the image facilitate a comparative analysis of two distinct categories of nanofiber membranes: CS/CQDs/PCL and Cd(II)@CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes [38]. The observed black curve reveals a significant crystalline peak at approximately 2θ ≈ 20°, which is indicative of the semicrystalline nature of polycaprolactone (PCL) within the unmodified nanofiber membrane. The green curve, which corresponds to the Cd(II)-modified membrane, exhibits a decrease in peak intensity around 20°. This observation indicates that the incorporation of Cd(II) ions interferes with the polymer's crystalline arrangement, causing in an increased amorphous nature of the substantial. The presence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) within both membrane classifications is characterized by the absence of clear crystalline peaks, suggesting their nature is primarily amorphous or nanocrystalline. This alteration in the diffraction pattern suggests that the incorporation of Cd(II) ions has a noteworthy effect on the crystallinity of the membrane. Such modifications could lead to improved material characteristics, making the membranes more effective for certain applications, including heavy metal adsorption, water purification, or sensing technologies. The alterations mentioned may influence the mechanical integrity, thermal resilience, and permeability of the membrane, thereby enhancing its suitability for diverse functional applications, as illustrated in Fig. 2(a).

The isotherm of N2 adsorption/desorption for the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane displays a Type IV isotherm, featuring a distinct hysteresis loop, which is characteristic of mesoporous materials. This behavior suggests the occurrence of capillary condensation within specifically defined pore structures, as exemplified in Fig. 2(b). The membrane displays a noteworthy surface area of 78.284 m2/g, which offers numerous active sites favorable to Cd(II) ions adsorption. Additionally, the measured pore radius of 2.44 nm indicates the presence of mesoporosity, which enhances ion diffusion and promotes swift adsorption kinetics, as illustrated in Fig. 2(c). The measured particle radius of 17.419 nm indicates a consistent dispersion of nanoparticles, which contributes to improved structural stability and a uniform pattern of adsorption behavior. The pore volume of 0.064559 cc/g indicates the presence of a highly developed porous structure, which enhances the membrane's ability to capture Cd(II) ions [39,40]. This increased capacity can be attributed to various mechanisms, including electrostatic interactions, complexation with functional groups such as -NH₂ from chitosan, and physical adsorption processes. The combined influence of chitosan's capacity for chelation and the superior electron transfer properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) mains to a notable increase in the efficiency of Cd(II) ions removal. These characteristic positions the membrane as a potent and sustainable option for the remediation of Cd(II) ions in water systems, as exemplified in Fig. 2(d).

The FT-IR spectrum of the CS/CQDs/PCL reveals significant information regarding the functional groups present, as well as their corresponding absorption wavenumbers. Notably, a prominent comprehensive absorption band located in the 3200–3450 cm−1 range specifies the attendance of OH stretching associated with chitosan and/or PCL, in addition to NH stretching related to chitosan. The presence of intramolecular hydrogen bonding inside chitosan is also suggested by this absorption characteristic. The absorption band observed approximately at 2935 cm−1 is associated with aliphatic CH groups, as exemplified in Fig. 2(e). The stretching vibration associated with the carbonyl group of the carboxylic component in polycaprolactone (PCL) was noticed at 1718 cm−1 [41]. Additionally, a band observed at 1614 cm−1 can be credited to the NH bending associated with the primary amine practical group present in chitosan. The presence of bands near 1425 and 1356 cm−1 provides compelling evidence for the CH2 bending and CH3 symmetrical deformations. Furthermore, it is evident that the asymmetric stretching of the C-O-C bridge is responsible for the absorption band at 1249 cm−1. Furthermore, the band at 1033 cm−1 is identified with confidence as corresponding to CN stretching. These findings conclusively illustrate the intricate molecular dynamics at play [42].

Fig. 2(f) represented a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) analysis of CQDs alongside elemental mapping from Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX). The SEM image at the center highlights the CQDs' surface morphology and spherical shape. Surrounding this are maps for Carbon (C), Nitrogen (N), and Oxygen (O). The Carbon map (C K ROI) shows green regions, indicating a high carbon concentration in the CQDs. The Nitrogen map (N K ROI) features green areas of lower intensity, signifying nitrogen for surface modification. The Oxygen map (O K ROI) in red illustrates oxygen distribution linked to CQDs' functional groups. Each map has a legend for intensity, aiding in the visualization of element concentration, with 1 mm scale bars as a reference.

The SEM image of the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane demonstrates a well-developed, interlaced fibrous structure characterized by randomly oriented nanofibers that possess a smooth and continuous morphology. This observation suggests that the electrospinning process was effectively executed and that the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are uniformly dispersed throughout the chitosan (CS) and polycaprolactone (PCL) composite matrix. The fibers demonstrate uniform diameters within the nanometer scale, which enhances the surface-to-volume ratio. Particularly when dealing with heavy metal ions like Cd(II) ions, this high ratio is crucial for maximizing the quantity of active sites accessible for adsorption. Intermittent bead-like formations are detected along the fibers, possibly resulting from localized variations in solution viscosity or the parameters of the electrospinning process. These formations can increase the surface roughness of the membrane, thereby enhancing its adsorption efficiency. The structural characteristics of the porous architecture, exemplified by the distinct voids present between the fibers, promote effective mass transfer and swift diffusion of targeted contaminants. Furthermore, the consistent distribution of fibers points to a strong mechanical integrity of the material [37,43]. This nanoscale configuration, in conjunction with the functional groups introduced by chitosan (CS) and the improved electron transfer capabilities of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), substantially enhances the membrane's efficacy for environmental cleanup, particularly in the context of Cd(II) ions elimination (Fig. 2(g)).

The SEM analysis of the Cd@CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane indicates a uniform distribution of elemental constituents throughout the membrane's surface, thereby certifying the effective combination of cadmium into the polymer matrix. The SEM micrograph reveals a compact, fibrous structure characterized by evenly distributed particles, indicating substantial interactions among the components, specifically chitosan (CS), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), polycaprolactone (PCL), and cadmium ions [44]. Furthermore, elemental mapping provides insights into the spatial arrangement of various elements: carbon (C, depicted in blue), nitrogen (N, represented in yellow), oxygen (O, shown in red), and cadmium (Cd, illustrated in cyan). Cadmium demonstrates a uniform distribution across the membrane, suggesting successful doping. Furthermore, the even dispersion of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen highlights the structural uniformity of the CS/CQDs/PCL matrix. The detection of nitrogen and oxygen supports the being of practical groups, such as –NH2 and carbonyl, which show a crucial character in the adsorption of heavy metals, as illustrated in Fig. 3 .

Fig. 2(f) represented an EDX analysis of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), including an EDX spectrum and a pie chart of elemental composition. The spectrum reveals prominent peaks for Carbon (C), Nitrogen (N), and Oxygen (O), with a notable peak at 0.3 keV indicating high Carbon concentration, followed by smaller peaks at 0.4 keV for Nitrogen and 0.5 keV for Oxygen. The pie chart quantifies the elements: Carbon at 61.1 % (red), Oxygen at 24.5 % (green), and Nitrogen at 14.4 % (dark red). This highlights the carbon-rich nature of the CQDs and suggests surface modifications for enhanced adsorption process.

Fig. 2(h) shows an EDX analysis of a CS/CQDs/PCL composite nanofiber membrane, including an EDX spectrum and a pie chart detailing its elemental composition. The spectrum reveals strong peaks for Carbon (C) around 0.2 keV, Nitrogen (N) at 0.4 keV, and Oxygen (O) at 0.6 keV, with Carbon being the most significant element, originating from the Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) and polycaprolactone (PCL). The lower Nitrogen peak is likely from chitosan (CS), while Oxygen indicates oxygen-containing functional groups from CQDs and PCL. The pie chart quantifies the elemental makeup: Carbon at 71.9 %, Nitrogen at 22.7 %, and Oxygen at 5.4 %. These results highlight the carbon dominance and the contribution of nitrogen from chitosan, indicating potential applications in drug delivery, tissue engineering, and environmental remediation.

The EDX analysis serves to enhance the findings from the SEM mapping by delivering quantitative data regarding elemental composition. The EDX spectrum displays pronounced peaks that correlate with the presence of cadmium, carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. Notably, cadmium emerges as the most significant element, constituting 76.9 % of the overall composition. The composition of the membrane includes oxygen at 10.9 %, carbon at 7.2 %, and nitrogen at 4.9 %, as illustrated in the provided pie chart [36]. The substantial quantity of cadmium present supports the notion of the membrane's effectiveness in cadmium capture. Additionally, the notable levels of oxygen and nitrogen indicate the presence of active functional groups, which are likely engaged in Cd(II) binding via processes including chelation and electrostatic interactions. The elemental makeup of the membrane demonstrates its capability for effective heavy metal adsorption, which is underpinned by its structural stability and chemical reactivity, as illustrated in Fig. 3.

The image presented features the C1s XPS spectrum associated with CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes, highlighting the analytical deconvolution of the C1s peak into three distinct components that signify different chemical states of carbon. The initial peak, located at around 285.0 eV (indicated in green), is linked to C-C/C-H bonds, which predominantly originate from polycaprolactone (PCL) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The secondary peak observed at approximately 286.5 eV (red) is related with C-O/C-N bonds, suggesting the appearance of hydroxyl (-OH) or amine (-NH2) practical groups, which are typical of chitosan (CS) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Furthermore, the tertiary peak located around 288.5 eV (purple) is credited to CO or O=C-O bonds, which implies the being of carboxyl (-COOH) or carbonyl (-C=O) groups. This is likely due to the ester functionalities in PCL or the surface oxidation processes occurring in CQDs. The identification of these three peaks signifies a successful integration of CS, CQDs, and PCL, implying an improvement in hydrophilicity and bioactivity attributed to the interactions among functional groups [45]. The associated image displays the C1s related to the Cd@CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes, showcasing the deconvoluted peaks identified within the core-level binding energy region of C1s. The horizontal axis illustrates binding energy measured in electron volts, varying from around 295 eV to 280 eV. In contrast, the vertical axis represents intensity, quantified in counts per second, indicating the detected number of photoelectrons. The spectrum exhibitions three significant peaks, each associated with diverse chemical states of carbon. The first peak, observed at around 285.0 eV (marked in green), is accredited to C-C/C-H bonds, indicative of PCL and CQDs. The following peak, located near 286.5 eV (represented in red), corresponds to C-O/C-N bonds, implying the being of hydroxyl (-OH) or amine (-NH2) groups, which are probably sourced from CS and functionalized CQDs. The third peak observed around 288.5 eV, illustrated in purple, is indicative of O=C-O or CO bonding, which suggests the attendance of carboxyl (-COOH) or carbonyl (-C=O) groups. These functional groups likely result from the surface oxidation of CQDs or the ester groups typical of PCL. The overall chemical structure implies notable functional interactions among CS, CQDs, PCL, and cadmium ions, thereby demonstrating the effective addition of cadmium in the nanofiber matrix, as depicted in Fig. 4 .

The presented image illustrates the O1s XPS spectrum associated with the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes, with a focus on the deconvoluted peaks within the O1s core-level binding energy range. The horizontal axis represents the binding energy measured in electron volts, covering a range from approximately 545 to 525 eV. In contrast, the vertical axis denotes the intensity, quantified in counts per second, which serves as a measure of the number of photoelectrons detected [46]. The spectrum displays two significant peaks, each corresponding to distinct chemical states of oxygen. The initial peak, located within the range of approximately 532.0–533.0 eV and represented by an orange hue, is predominantly associated with oxygen present in carbonyl (C=O) or hydroxyl (OC) linkages. This association likely stems from the ester groups in PCL as well as the surface-modified CQDs. The observed peak suggests the existence of hydroxyl (-OH) or ether (O) functional groups, which are recognized for their role in increasing hydrophilicity. This increase in hydrophilicity may significantly influence both the biodegradability and bioactivity of the material under consideration. The second peak, observed in the range of approximately 530.5 to 531.5 eV (highlighted in green), is indicative of the presence of oxygen linked to metal-oxide (M-O) bonds, carboxyl (-COOH) groups, or adsorbed oxygen species. This observation implies a potential surface oxidation of CQDs or suggests that there may be chemical interactions occurring between CS and the CQDs. This observation reinforces the likelihood of substantial hydrogen bonding or possible interactions with metal ions, which could enhance the stability of the nanofibers. Additionally, these factors may also augment their antimicrobial effectiveness and ability to eliminate the Cd(II). The O1s XPS spectrum of the Cd@CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes reveals three clearly deconvoluted peaks. This observation suggests that there is an effective incorporation of cadmium alongside oxygen-containing functional groups within the nanofiber structure. The initial peak, noted at approximately 533.0 eV (red), corresponds to the CO (carbonyl) and OC (hydroxyl) bonds [35]. This peak predominantly originates from the ester groups found in PCL and hydroxyl-functionalized CQDs. These findings indicate a significant presence of strong hydrophilic interactions. The secondary peak, located around 531.5 eV (indicated in green), is associated with cadmium‑oxygen (CdO) and cadmium-hydroxyl (Cd-OH) bond formations. This observation underscores the coordination of the metal with the polymer, facilitated by interactions involving carboxyl (-COOH) groups, which may contribute to improved antimicrobial and catalytic functionalities. The tertiary peak, found near 530.0 eV (shown in blue), signifies either lattice oxygen or absorbed oxygen species. This finding suggests the presence of oxidation-reduction phenomena and surface connections within the composite substantial (Fig. 4).

The XPS examination of the nitrogen 1 s (N1s) area in the membrane reveals two distinct peaks after deconvolution. The first peak, around 398–399 eV, indicates nitrogen from amine (-NH2) or imine (C=N) groups, linked to chitosan or CQDs, showing nitrogen bonded to carbon in a conjugated electronic environment. The second peak, detected in the 400–401 eV range, is linked to protonated amine groups (-NH3 +) or the presence of graphitic nitrogen. This indicates that the nitrogen exists within a more oxidized or positively charged chemical setting. Such a condition is likely a result of interactions with PCL or the surface functionalization processes applied to CQDs. The comparative intensity and surface area of each spectral peak indicate a greater prevalence of nitrogen present as amine or imine groups rather than as protonated amine or graphitic nitrogen. Additionally, the minor shifts and broadening observed in the binding energy provide insights into fluctuations in the local chemical environment, which may arise from influences like hydrogen bonding, conjugation effects, or interactions with the polymer matrix. The XPS spectrum examination of the N1s region in the Cd@CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane reveals two separate peaks following deconvolution. This observation suggests the presence of varied nitrogen chemical environments that are affected by the introduction of cadmium. The initial peak noticed in the spectrum, found between 398 and 399 eV, is indicative of nitrogen associated with amine (-NH2) or imine (C=N) functional groups. These groups are typically found in CS as well as in CQDs. This implies a direct bond between nitrogen and carbon within a conjugated framework, which may play a role in coordinating with cadmium ions. The second peak, identified in the energy range of 400–401 eV, is associated with protonated amine groups (-NH3 +) or graphitic nitrogen. This association indicates that nitrogen resides in a more oxidized or positively charged state, potentially influenced by its interactions with PCL. Additionally, the presence of cadmium in the environment may induce electron withdrawal effects, further contributing to the observed peak characteristics. The red deconvolution curves, along with the green baseline, exemplify the processes of background subtraction and peak fitting in the analysis. The areas of the identified peaks correspond to the relative nitrogen concentrations present in each specified state. The observed intensity and minor broadening of these peaks imply alterations in the nitrogen environment as a result of cadmium complexation, which subsequently induces variations in binding energy [47]. This finding validates the successful interaction between nitrogen-functional groups incorporated in the nanofiber matrix and cadmium ions, as illustrated in Fig. 4.

The XPS analysis of the Cd3d region within the Cd@CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane reveals the attendance of two prominent peaks. These peaks resemble to the spin-orbit doublets of Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2, generally observed at binding energies near 411–412 and 404–405 eV, individually. This observation aligns with the expected energy separation of about 6–7 eV, which is characteristic of these electronic states. The peaks, which have been deconvoluted and are represented in blue and red, underscore the existence of Cd(II) ions residing in varying chemical environments. The primary peaks indicate a coordination of cadmium with functional groups containing nitrogen or oxygen, which originate from CS and CQDs. In contrast, the secondary green peaks are likely indicative of CdO or CdN bonds that arise from interactions involving PCL and CS. The red fitting curves and green baseline illustrate the efficacy of peak deconvolution, where the relative areas of the peaks correspond to the concentrations of cadmium present in various states [48]. The observed minor broadening and asymmetry of the peaks indicate the existence of diverse bonding environments, thereby affirming the successful integration of cadmium into the nanofiber matrix along with significant interactions with the functional groups found in CS/CQDs/PCL, as showed in Fig. 4.

The XPS survey spectrum provides a comparative analysis of the surface elemental composition between the nanofiber membranes CS/CQDs/PCL (represented by the black line) and Cd@CS/CQDs/PCL (indicated by the red line), elucidating significant alterations consequent to cadmium incorporation. Both spectra exhibit pronounced peaks related with C 1 s (∼285 eV), O 1 s (∼532 eV), and N 1 s (∼400 eV), which are indicative of the presence of CS, CQDs, and PCL. This observation reaffirms the presence of key practical groups comprising carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen in these materials. The spectrum of Cd@CS/CQDs/PCL exhibits a notable supplementary peak near 405 eV, which corresponds to the Cd 3d5 orbital. This observation serves as evidence for the effective integration of cadmium within the nanofiber matrix. Furthermore, the heightened intensity of the N 1 s peak observed in the Cd-modified sample indicates an increase in nitrogen coordination, which may be attributed to interactions between cadmium and nitrogen (CdN). The observed variations in binding energies and differences in peak intensities across the two spectra suggest modifications in the chemical situation, indicative of robust interactions among Cd(II) ions and the practical groups present in CS and CQDs. Furthermore, the heightened background in the Cd@CS/CQDs/PCL spectrum implies surface alteration due to cadmium integration, resulting in changes to the electronic structure. This analysis substantiates the successful doping of cadmium while maintaining the intrinsic polymeric architecture of CS/CQDs/PCL, as illustrated in Fig. 4.

Fig. 5(a) presents the zero point of charge (pHzpc) for the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane, which is identified as 4.59. This value signifies the pH level where the membrane's surface achieves a net neutral charge, indicating an equivalency in the quantity of positive and negative surface charges. The graph delineates the association among the change in pH (ΔpH) and the initial (pHi), clearly indicating that ΔpH intersects the zero line exactly at a pH of 4.59. At pH levels lower than this specified value, the surface of the membrane exhibits a positive charge, as evidenced by the positive ΔpH values [49]. Conversely, at pH levels exceeding this threshold, the surface charge transitions to a negative, as indicated by negative ΔpH values. This transition from a positive to a negative charge at the membrane surface is pivotal in influencing the membrane's interactions with charged species. Lower than the zero point of charge (pHzpc), specifically when the pH is below 4.59, the membrane exhibits a repulsive interaction with cationic species, such as Cd(II) ions. This repulsion is attributed to electrostatic forces, leading to a diminished efficacy in the adsorption procedure. However, the membrane's surface acquires a negative charge when the operational pH rises beyond 4.59. This promotes electrostatic attraction, which improves the adsorption of positively charged Cd(II) ions. This phenomenon highlights the membrane's capability in applications aimed at the removal of heavy metals.

Fig. 5(b) demonstrations how pH levels affect the effectiveness of the adsorption of Cd(II) ions onto the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane. It is obvious that the removal of Cd(II) ions rises as the pH of the solution increases from 2.0 to 6.0, with the peak adsorption occurring at a pH of 6.0. Conversely, lower pH levels are detrimental to this process, primarily due to the presence of H+ ions, which effectively inhibit the adsorption by masking the metal ions. Moreover, an elevation in pH appears to result in a diminished removal efficiency, likely attributable to the synthesis of metal hydroxides and subsequent chemical precipitation. The CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane demonstrated a peak adsorption capacity for Cd(II) ions, recorded at 279.2 mg/g. Comprehensive adsorption investigations were conducted on the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane, specifically at a pH value of 6.0, focusing on the adsorption attributes for Cd(II) ions [50].

The work examines the adsorption efficiency of Cd(II) on a CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane by systematically changing the sorbent dosage since 0.02 to 0.5 g within a 25 mL test solution. The initial concentration was consistently maintained at 300 mg/L, with the pH set to 6 and a fixed interaction time of 100 min, as illustrated in Fig. 5(c). The results specify that the sorption of Cd(II) ions increased by an elevated dose of the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane. However, this enhancement in sorption efficiency reached a saturation point at approximately 97.3 % when the dosage was escalated to 0.5 g [51]. An analysis of the observed phenomenon indicates that sorption efficiency is suboptimal due to an imperfect number of active places existing for the binding of cadmium ions at lower doses of the CS/CQDs/PCL. As the dosage of the nanofiber membrane is elevated, there is a corresponding increase in the adsorption of Cd(II) ions, leading to enhanced sorption efficiency until the system reaches a point of saturation. Notably, a dosage of 0.02 g of the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane achieved the maximum adsorption capability, quantified at 225 mg/g. Consequently, for subsequent experiments, a quantity of 0.02 g of the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane was employed, as represented in Fig. 5(c).

Fig. 5(d) illustrates the equilibrium adsorption work of Cd(II) ions on the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane, with a focus on concentration dependency measured in mg/L. Initially, there is a rapid increase in ion adsorption; however, as concentration continues to rise, the adsorption rate begins to decline. To achieve a comprehensive understanding of ion adsorption onto CS/CQDs/PCL, two distinct adsorption isotherm equations were employed. An adsorption isotherm represents the correlation among the amount of solute adsorbed each unit mass of the adsorbent besides the concentration of that solute at equilibrium, all while preserving a constant temperature. The phenomena of adsorption are elucidated through the application of various isotherm models. Notably, the Freundlich and Langmuir isotherms emerge as the most significant frameworks, as they offer critical theoretical insights into the adsorption procedure and elucidate the intrinsic characteristics of adsorption behavior [52].

Fig. 5(e) shows an investigation into the temporal dynamics of the adsorption of Cd(II) ions on the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane, with an original Cd(II) ions concentration of 400 mg/L. It is detected that the adsorption capacity exhibited an upward trend and remained stable for a duration of 100 min. Notably, when compared to the current membrane materials, fibrous ions or membrane demonstrate superior kinetic performance in the adsorption process. The data specifies that the attachment of metal ions to the active places on the adsorbent significantly intensified until reaching 100 min, at which point these ions appeared to uniformly saturate the surface. It is suggested that this process reflects a balance among the adsorption/desorption of metal ions during this occurrence. The CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane demonstrated a remarkable ability to adsorb Cd(II) ions, indicating a significant affinity for these ions. The quantified adsorption capacity achieved for Cd(II) reached 341.8 mg/g. This phenomenon may be credited to numerous factors, with the solution pH and the inherent affinity characteristics of the nanofiber membranes [53].

Within the range of 20 to 45 °C, the experiment assessed the impact of temperature. Temperature was found to positively correlate with the removal efficacy of Cd(II) ions, as illustrated in Fig. 5(f). Temperatures above 25 °C were found to increase clearing efficiency. The existence of unstable hydrogen-bonded interactions between the adsorbent besides adsorbate could be the cause of this behavior. This instability facilitates the migration of Cd(II) ions from the solid phase towards the bulk phase through the adsorption procedure [54].

The request of adsorption isotherm models aids as a critical analytical tool for explaining the adsorption characteristics of Cd(II) ions on CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes. This approach provides valuable insights into the adsorption mechanisms, thereby facilitating improvements in the performance of the material. The Langmuir isotherm model serves a critical character in quantifying the maximum adsorption capability while evaluating the affinity of Cd(II) for the nanofiber surface, suggesting a uniform adsorption process occurring on a limited number of places [55]. Conversely, the Freundlich isotherm provides a deeper understanding of heterogeneous adsorption, highlighting the intricate characteristics of the membrane structure and its surface belongings [56]. The Dubinin–Radushkevich isotherm helps decide between physical besides chemical adsorption, crucial for optimizing CS/CQDs/PCL nanofibers and enhancing Cd(II) ion removal efficiency [57]. Conversely, the Temkin isotherm assesses interactions between adsorbate molecules, guiding modifications to boost membrane performance [58]. The Jossens isotherm demonstrates versatility in modeling data from diverse adsorption situations, effectively addressing non-linear relationships within intricate wastewater compositions (Table S4). This flexibility enriches the comprehension of Cd(II) adsorption dynamics, allowing for the determination of ideal conditions to achieve peak efficiency. The utilization of various models provides a comprehensive considerate of the adsorption properties of Cd(II) on CS/CQDs/PCL membranes [58].

The use of adsorption isotherm models, notably the Langmuir model, to examine the Cd(II) ions adsorption on CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes offers valuable insights into the adsorption device and the performance of the adsorbent. The Langmuir model indicates a constant (KL) of 0.02 (L/mg) and a separation factor (RL) of 0.36, which provide information about the adsorption dynamics, indicating that a small number of identical sites make up the surface on which the adsorption occurs. The (KL) value indicates the affinity of Cd(II) ions for the surface of the nanofibers; a lower (KL) value implies a moderate strength of interaction, suggesting that adsorption may be highly effective under favorable conditions. The (RL) value, which varieties from 0 to 1, signifies a favorable interaction for the adsorption of Cd(II) on the membranes. This metric is crucial for evaluating the practicality of utilizing these nanofiber membranes in the process of cadmium removal from wastewater. By using the Langmuir isotherm, researchers can determine the maximum adsorption capacity, which is a crucial factor in determining the adsorbent's effectiveness, as exemplified in Fig. 6(a). The model's simplicity enables easy computations and interpretations, enhancing the optimization of operational parameters like contact duration, concentration, and temperature for efficiency. Insights from the model guide modifications to improve nanofiber membranes' structural and chemical properties [55].

The application of adsorption isotherm models, notably the Freundlich model, serves to elucidate the adsorption behavior of Cd(II) ions of CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes. This approach contributes to a deeper comprehension of the dynamics and efficiency of the adsorption procedure. The Freundlich model, characterized by constants of (n) at 2.37 and (KF) at 41.62 (mg/g)(L/mg)1/n, effectively delineates the dynamics of heterogeneous adsorption on substrates exhibiting varying affinities. A value of (n) exceeding 1 signifies advantageous conditions, implying a robust affinity of chitosan/graphene quantum dots/polycaprolactone for cadmium ions (Cd(II)), thereby enhancing the efficiency of the adsorption mechanism [56]. The magnitude of (KF) reflects the membranes' proficiency in holding considerable quantities of cadmium, which is pivotal for evaluating the practical utility of these materials in the setting of water treatment. The model delineates the non-linear adsorption characteristics, which are pertinent in situations involving fluctuating cadmium concentrations in polluted water. It facilitates the optimization of critical parameters, including contact duration, initial Cd(II) concentration, and temperature, all of which are crucial for achieving optimal adsorption efficiency. The insights derived from the model provide a framework for adjusting the nanofiber membranes to improve their adsorption capabilities. In summary, employing the Freundlich model to analyze the adsorption of Cd(II) on CS/CQDs/PCL membranes enriches the comprehension of the adsorption dynamics, pinpoints the most favorable conditions for effective pollutant extraction, and aids in the development of sustainable approaches for addressing heavy metal pollution in wastewater, as illustrated in Fig. 6(a).

The use of adsorption isotherm models, particularly the Dubinin–Radushkevich (D-R) model, facilitates the examination of Cd(II) ions adsorption onto CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes, thereby improving the comprehension of the associated dynamics and mechanisms. The D-R model includes parameters such as the adsorption capability (QDR) of 326.7 mg/g, the affinity coefficient (KDR) of 6.7E-5 mol2/kJ2, and the adsorption energy (Ea) of 33.4 kJ/mol, which offer valuable information regarding adsorption capacities and energies. The notable value of the quantitative distribution ratio (QDR) emphasizes the membranes' exceptional capability in sequestering cadmium ions, which is crucial for effective wastewater management (Table S5). Meanwhile, the kinetic distribution ratio (KDR) delivers visions into the energy of adsorption involved in the procedure. The activation energy (Ea) measurement of 33.4 kJ/mol further indicates that chemisorption is the prevailing mechanism, signifying the establishment of chemical bonds between cadmium ions and the practical groups present in the nanofibers. This comprehension enhances nanofiber membrane design for improved cadmium removal. The Dubinin-Radushkevich (D-R) model clearly outlines physical in addition to chemical adsorption mechanisms, guiding material selection and processing methods. It assesses factors like temperature and cadmium concentration on adsorption behavior, optimizing operational parameters for better efficiency. Overall, the D-R model provides insights into adsorption dynamics, identifies optimal conditions for pollutant removal, and supports sustainable heavy metal management in wastewater systems [57].

Employing the models of adsorption isotherm like the Temkin model to examine the adsorption of Cd(II) ions onto (CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes facilitates a deeper considerate of the adsorption phenomena. Specifically, the Temkin model reveals significant parameters, with a constant (bT) reported as 29.03 (J/mol) and (KT) as 0.34 (L/mol), which elucidates the nature of cadmium's interaction with the composite membranes. The (bT) value serves as an indicator of the heat associated with adsorption, implying that there are favorable interactions that enhance the efficiency of cadmium removal. Meanwhile, the (KT) constant provides a quantitative measure of the adsorption capacity of CS/CQDs/PCL membranes for Cd(II). This metric is essential for optimizing numerous limits such as original cadmium concentration, interaction time, and temperature to achieve maximum adsorption efficacy. The Temkin model enables the evaluation of a consistent distribution of binding energy, which influences the adsorption process at varying concentrations of cadmium. Additionally, it assists in recognizing instances of competitive adsorption when several contaminants coexist, thereby informing improvements to nanofiber membranes for enhanced efficacy [58].

Using models for adsorption isotherms, particularly the Jossens model, significantly improves the examination of Cd(II) ions adsorption onto CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes. The Jossens model, defined by constants (K) of 8.81 and (J) of 0.0076, accurately reflects the adsorption dynamics under a variety of conditions, taking into account the non-linear interactions present in intricate wastewater scenarios. The (K) value serves as a crucial indicator of a robust adsorption capability, underscoring the pronounced affinity exhibited by the CS/CQDs/PCL membranes towards Cd(II) ions, which is essential for successful pollutant remediation. In contrast, the (J) parameter provides a measure of the adsorption process's intensity, facilitating a deeper empathetic of the connections occurring between Cd(II) ions and the nanofibers surfaces. Utilizing the Jossens model enables researchers to analyze the variations in adsorption characteristics across diverse conditions. This understanding aids in the development of optimization strategies pertaining to key limits, with original cadmium concentration, contact time, and temperature. Additionally, these findings enhance the ability to compare the performance of CS/CQDs/PCL membranes with various other adsorbents, thereby refining the evaluation process. Utilizing the Jossens model to study Cd(II) ions adsorption on CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes offers valuable insights into adsorption dynamics. This method helps identify optimal conditions for pollutant removal, improving strategies for sustainable heavy metal management in wastewater treatment [58].

The application of varied kinetic models, specifically Pseudo-First-order [59], Pseudo-Second-order [60], Elovich, and Intraparticle diffusion [61] models, to examine the adsorption of Cd(II) ions onto CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes, shows a critical character in elucidating the underlying adsorption dynamics and the overall efficacy of the adsorbent [62]. The Pseudo-First-order model is chiefly effective in characterizing the early stages of adsorption, thereby facilitating a rapid evaluation of Cd(II) ions removal rates, which is pivotal for the implementation of real-time wastewater treatment methodologies. This model underscores the membranes' proficiency in efficiently sequestering cadmium ions in a swift manner. Conversely, the Pseudo-Second-order model investigates the underlying mechanisms of adsorption, suggesting a chemisorption process founded on the premise that the reaction rate is comparative to the square of the unfilled places. This analysis provides insight into the maximum adsorption capability and the nature of the interactions at play (Table S4). The Intraparticle diffusion model elucidates the transport mechanisms of Cd(II) ions within the membranes by differentiating between limitations posed by external mass transfer and the internal diffusion of cadmium ions into the nanofibers. Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for identifying potential bottlenecks and enhancing the properties of the membranes. Finally, the Elovich model provides significant benefits for heterogeneous surfaces by accommodating the differences in adsorption site availability and the gradual process of saturation. This aspect of the model is particularly useful in analyzing how adsorption rates evolve over time, especially in scenarios where cadmium concentrations vary. Utilizing a variety of kinetic models enables researchers to advance a comprehensive vision into the adsorption procedure of Cd(II) on CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes. This approach facilitates the determination of ideal parameters that enhance dye removal efficiency, thereby improving the design of the adsorbent. In summary, these kinetic models bolster the credibility of the results, contributing to the formulation of effective techniques for addressing heavy metal pollution within wastewater treatment, as demonstrated in Fig. 6(b).

The application of the Pseudo-First-order kinetic model to examine the adsorption of Cd(II) ions on CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes provides a deeper considerate of the adsorption kinetics and overall effectiveness. This model is defined by a rate constant (K1) of 0.28 (min−1) x 10−2, accurately representing the initial phases of the adsorption procedure and highlighting the quick uptake of Cd(II) ions. Such insights are crucial for optimizing wastewater treatment processes. The (K1) value serves as a quantitative metric for researchers to evaluate the rate of adsorption, demonstrating that CS/CQDs/PCL membranes exhibit the capacity to effectively sequester considerable quantities of Cd(II) within a brief time frame. The model's straightforward nature aids in the computation and refinement of many parameters, such as contact duration and original concentration, thereby enhancing overall adsorption efficiency [59]. Additionally, the findings derived from the model contribute to the enhancement of nanofiber membranes, focusing on property modifications that facilitate improved adsorption capabilities. This methodology facilitates a comparative analysis of Cd(II) ions adsorption on CS/CQDs/PCL membranes against other adsorbent materials, thereby offering an in-depth assessment of their relative effectiveness (Table S6).

The request of kinetic models, notably the Pseudo-Second-order model, to examine the adsorption characteristics of Cd(II) ions on CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes offers a deeper vision into the adsorption dynamics. This model, characterized by a rate constant (K2) of 5.04E-5 (g.mg−1 min−1) x 10−2, effectively captures the kinetics of the process, particularly when chemical connections play a important role in the adsorption device [60]. The model indicates that the rate of adsorption correlates with the square of the vacant sites, which underscores the predominance of chemisorption. This provides significant insights into the interactions between cadmium ions and the practical groups existent on the membrane. Additionally, the low value of (K2) assists researchers in evaluating the effectiveness of CS/CQDs/PCL membranes in sequestering cadmium ions from solutions. This model is instrumental in assessing the maximum adsorption capability, which is essential for understanding the retention of cadmium by membranes at equilibrium. It facilitates the optimization of various parameters, with contact time, initial cadmium concentration, in addition to temperature, to improve the effectiveness of the adsorption process.

Employing kinetic models like the Intraparticle Diffusion Model in the examination of Cd(II) ions adsorption onto CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes delivers a more profound knowledge of the adsorption dynamics. This model elucidates the transport mechanisms of Cd(II) ions, helping to regulate whether the rates of adsorption are primarily influenced by external mass transfer processes or by intraparticle diffusion phenomena [61]. The parameter (Ki) of 37.18 (mg.g−1 min1/2) indicates a significant rate of intraparticle diffusion, reflecting the efficient movement of cadmium ions into the nanofiber. This capability is vital for facilitating rapid adsorption and removal of contaminants. Furthermore, the maximum adsorption capability (X) of 148.8 (mg/g) underscores the effectiveness of the membranes in sequestering cadmium, which is a critical factor in the treatment of wastewater. The model contributes to the analysis of kinetic factors across temporal sequences, allowing for the optimization of variables such as contact duration and cadmium concentration to increase overall efficiency. Furthermore, the insights derived from the model can inform alterations in membrane design, aimed at enhancing their structural characteristics and adsorption capabilities.

Using kinetic models, particularly the Elovich model, to examine the adsorption performance of Cd(II) ion on CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes provides significant visions into the dynamics of adsorption and the efficacy of the adsorbent substantial. The Elovich model, which is defined by its parameters (beta) at 108.8 (g/mg) and (alpha) at 0.0023 (mg.g−1 min−1), accurately represents processes occurring on heterogeneous surfaces where the obtainability of adsorption sites fluctuates. The (beta) parameter signifies the peak adsorption capacity for Cd(II), which is crucial for assessing the effectiveness of membranes used in wastewater treatment applications. The (alpha) parameter indicates the initial rate of adsorption, underscoring that the absorption of Cd(II) takes place in a gradual manner. This gradual process is crucial for analyzing kinetic behaviors across various operational conditions. Furthermore, the model takes into account the effects of surface coverage on these rates, which is significant when dealing with varying concentrations of cadmium. Utilizing the Elovich model improves the prediction accuracy concerning the belongings of contact time, original cadmium concentration, and temperature on adsorption efficiency. The insights derived from this model facilitate comparative analyses with alternative systems, thereby deepening the understanding of the performance of CS/CQDs/PCL membranes in relation to various adsorbent materials [62].

Three separate phases of Cd(II) ions adsorption enabled by CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes were delineated by using the kinetic information to calibrate an intraparticle diffusion model (Fig. 6(c)). The external diffusion of heavy metal ions, which are absorbed and then anchored to the surface, primarily controls the adsorption mechanism in the first phase. The absorption sites present in the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes facilitate rapid adsorption during this phase [63]. The subsequent phase of the procedure is characterized by intraparticle diffusion, a process in which metal ions are slowly absorbed by both the surface and the inner pores of the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes. In the final phase, the adsorption places of the adsorbent approach their maximum capacity, resulting in a diminished capacity to capture and retain further heavy metal ions. Consequently, the rate of adsorption exhibits minimal variation, ultimately stabilizing at a state of equilibrium, as illustrated in Fig. 6(c).

A thorough investigation was carried out using the given equations to look into how temperature affected the absorption of Cd(II) ions between 293 and 318 K. Numerous parameters were calculated as part of this investigation, including the activation energy (Ea), the Gibbs free energy (ΔGo), the entropy change (ΔSo), and the variation in enthalpy (ΔHo) [64]. Since the ΔGo values are always negative at all temperature ranges (Fig. 7(a)), the adsorption of Cd(II) ions is spontaneous. Furthermore, as the temperature increases, there is a noticeable rise in the magnitude of the negative ΔGo values (Fig. 7(b)). This trend indicates that entropy shows a beneficial character in the adsorption of Cd(II), thereby enhancing the spontaneity of the procedure at higher temperatures, as evidenced in Fig. 7(c). The entropy change, represented by ΔSo at 294.9 J/mol.K, specifies an increase in disorder at the solid-solution boundary throughout the adsorption of Cd(II) ions. Furthermore, the enthalpy change, ΔHo [65], measured at 86.45 kJ/mol.K, specifies a positive value, suggesting that the procedure is endothermic [66]. According to Table S4, this advises that the adsorption of Cd(II) ions is mostly chemical. As stated to Table S7, the Arrhenius equation produced an adsorption energy of 36.2 kJ/mol (Table S8). This finding supports the conclusion that the adsorption mechanism can be classified as chemisorption [63].

The efficiency of adsorbing heavy metals, specifically Cd(II) ions, is significantly determined by the practical groups existing on the adsorbent surface and the characteristics of the surrounding environment. The fundamental processes involved in adsorption are contingent upon the specific materials employed. Investigations into the adsorption behavior of Cd(II) by a membrane composed of chitosan (CS), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and polycaprolactone (PCL) reveal that the mechanisms of adsorption can be confidential into two principal classes: chemical and physical adsorption. Physical adsorption is characterized by relatively weak interactions, primarily driven by electrostatic forces like van der Waals interactions. This type of attraction takes place between the charged ions within the solution and the protonated functional groups namely amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups found in CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane. This method secures the heavy metal onto the surface of the adsorbent by electrostatic connections rather than strong chemical bonds. On the other hand, stronger and longer-lasting interactions are involved in chemical adsorption, also known as chemisorption. Ion exchange, in which ions on the surface of the adsorbent replace ions in the solution. Surface complexation is the process by which heavy metal ions combine with surface functional groups to produce stable chemical compounds. Chemisorption can occur through a variety of methods, including redox reactions, in which the adsorbed molecules' chemical states are altered by electron transfer. Ion exchange is essential to these activities because it makes it easier to remove Cd(II) through the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane. The phenomenon of surface complexation is defined by the interactions that occur between the heavy metal ions in question and the surface functional groups, specifically the hydroxyl, carboxyl, or amine groups found on chitosan/PCL nanofibers. The connections under consideration lead to the creation of chemical complexes that exhibit significantly enhanced strength and stability relative to mere physical adsorption. It is noteworthy that collaborative adsorption typically encompasses a combination of both physical and chemical mechanisms. Cd(II) ions can interact with carboxyl groups existent on the adsorbent surface through electrostatic attraction. Additionally, they may associate with hydroxyl groups through hydrogen bonding mechanisms, and establish connections with amino groups via a complexation process.

During the adsorption procedure, the interactions among the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane, and the adsorbate Cd(II) ions are enhanced through various intermolecular forces, with van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonding, and π-π interactions, which is a particular form of non-covalent bonding concerning aromatic structures (refer to Fig. 8 ). The elevated polar surface area of the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane significantly contributes to strengthening these interactions, thereby enhancing its overall adsorption capacity. Moreover, electrostatic interactions play a vital character, predominantly at a pH of 6, where the presence of Cd(II) ions exhibits a marked affinity for the marginally negatively charged CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane. This phenomenon results in an increase in both the adsorption capability and efficiency of the membrane. Notably, the carbon‑nitrogen (CN) bond present within the carbon quantum dots structure significantly contributes to the enhancement of these interactions (Fig. 8). The strong framework and effective adsorption capability of the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane position it as a highly viable material for the elimination of heavy metals. A significant body of research indicates that the adsorption dynamics of Cd(II) onto the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane correspond closely with the model of Langmuir adsorption. This model presupposes a consistent distribution of active places across the surface of the adsorbent. The model of Freundlich is relevant in certain instances, particularly when considering a surface characterized by heterogeneity. Furthermore, the adsorption process's dynamics follow the pseudo-second-order kinetic model, demonstrating that chemical adsorption is responsible for the rate-limiting stage in this case.

In this study, a 50 mL solution comprising 100 mg/L of Cd(II) ions underwent treatment with 0.02 g of a composite nanofiber membrane made from CS/CQDs/PCL at a pH value of 6 for a duration of 100 min, facilitating the adsorption procedure. After filtration, the resultant Cd(II)-saturated absorbent was exposed to 50 mL of a 0.05 mol/L ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) solution, which was subjected to continuous agitation for 4 h at a controlled temperature of 298.15 K. Centrifugation aided as the technique for separating the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane from the mixture, followed by a washing step using distilled water. Subsequent to drying the membrane in a vacuum environment at a temperature of 333 K, it was reintroduced for use in the subsequent cycle. The processes of adsorption and repetition were executed a full of five times. The pronounced ability of the sorbent to undergo reactivation is important for its potential commercial applicability [46,67]. Across five successive sequences of adsorption-desorption, the retention capacities demonstrated a decline, with values measuring 98.6, 97.2, 94.8, 90.6, and 84.2 % of the initial capacity, respectively. The decline in uptake observed with an increasing number of sequences can be ascribed to the loss of active places and alterations in the specific geometric configuration of the material. These results suggest that the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane exhibits potential for effective recycling. To assess the stability of the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane, XRD patterns were analyzed before and after the regeneration process, as illustrated in Fig. 9(a). The comparison settles that the structural integrity of the adsorbent remained unchanged even after suffering five cycles [68–70]. As shown in Fig. 9(b), the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane exhibits the capacity to be used for up to five cycles. According to the research, the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane's infrared spectra do not significantly alter after being exposed to water for 300 min. This finding implies that the adsorbent remains stable during the testing phase [71].

As shown in Table S9, the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane's capacity to adsorb Cd(II) ions contaminants was assessed in relation to a number of powder and membrane sorbents. This analysis emphasizes the difficulties in comparing various adsorbents because of their disparate physical shapes, with films, nanofibers, and powders, as well as the particular experimental setups used for each evaluation [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response surface analysis and experimental design modeling

ce:section.ce:para:
The results of the analysis of variance (ANOVA), a rigorous statistical technique used to evaluate the validity of the investigation carried out, are presented in Table 1 . An F-value of 51.69 and a p-value of <0.0001 are determined for the Cd(II) ions removal model [72–74]. This outcome signifies that the Box-Behnken Design (BBD) model demonstrates a substantial degree of statistical meaning. Moreover, the association coefficient (R2 = 0.985) indicates a significant consistency among the expected and actual measurements of Cd(II) ions, as outlined in Table 2 . The parameters of the BBD are deemed statistically noteworthy once the p-value falls below 0.05 under the defined situations (Table 3 ). The relationship among the variables being analyzed and the resulting answer can be described using a second-degree polynomial, as indicated in Eq. (5). Furthermore, Eq. In this system, a code of +1 is typically used to indicate higher levels, whereas a code of −1 is used to indicate lower levels. This coded equation aids as a crucial tool for determining the relative importance of the variables, allowing for study by carefully examining the coefficients associated with each component [75].

Based on preset values for every factor, predictions for the results can be generated using the equation that is supplied regarding particular concrete factors. The proper original units must be used to communicate these data. However, using this equation to compare the relative effects of each component requires caution. This limitation arises because the coefficients are adjusted to meet the relevant units of the components because the intercept does not match the center of the design space [33].

A notable reaction is revealed by the experimental data, which are characterized by a pure error-related non-significant lack of fit. This finding suggests that the Box-Behnken Design (BBD) model is an excellent way to forecast the elimination of Cd(II) ions from the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane surface within the given parameter range [63,76]. The relationship between adsorbent dosage and pH demonstrated statistical significance when both temperature and constant time were controlled, as indicated by P-values falling less 0.0500. This suggests that the model terms hold relevance, as illustrated in Table 3.

The relationship between expected and observed values is shown in Fig. 10 (a–f). The removal of Cd(II) ions was carried out utilizing the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane. The effectiveness of this elimination strategy was evaluated using a number of analytical techniques, including perturbation plots, highly studentized residuals, the BOX-COX Plot, and residual vs. run analysis [77,78]. The notable alignment observed between the anticipated and the observed standards in Figs. 10(a, b) underscores the statistical reliability of the model employed. Additionally, Figs. 10(c, d) elucidate the association among the residuals and the adsorption procedure of Cd(II) [32]. The model's efficacy and reliability are indicated by the wide distribution of data facts surrounding the zero benchmark, coupled with their gathering in that vicinity. This suggests that the model could potentially have missed identifying any unrecognized or atypical values or trends, which could be revealed through a comparative analysis of the predicted response values against the actual observed values [41]. The Box-Cox curves exposed in Fig. 10(e) provide an efficient way to choose the best influence transformation purpose for the response variable. In the modified model, Lambda is the lowest point on the Box-Cox graph, representing the least residual sum of squares. This point is the optimal value of Lambda, which is the minimum residual sum of squares for the modified model [33,37]. The graphics that illustrate the model's applicability show that it performs admirably. One variable was altered while the values of every other variable were the same in order to assess the yield response. The alterations illustrated in the figure indicate that length, pH, and dosage exert the most significant influence on the geometric configuration. In contrast, all variables appear to positively affect the adsorption capability. Furthermore, the association among pH levels and adsorption process length demonstrates how quickly these variables affect adsorption capability [79,80]. According to the findings exposed in Fig. 10(f), the adsorption capability is positively impacted by time, pH, and dose through the procedure. Additionally, as exposed in Fig. 10(f), the ideal adsorption parameters were originate to be a pH of 6, a maximum amount of 0.02 g, and a interaction time of 100 min [81].

Three-dimensional Plots of surface response were employed to analyze the significant relationships among the various components involved. This approach facilitated a comprehensive understanding of how these elements influence the elimination of Cd(II) ions. Fig. 11(a) exemplifies the influence of deviations in pH and the dosage of CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membranes on the adsorption capacity for Cd(II), with each experimental condition being maintained for a duration of 100 min. Fig. 11(b) exemplifies a noteworthy trend wherein the adsorption capacity for Cd(II) exhibits an upward trajectory as the pH level is elevated from 2 to 6. The optimal settings for this adsorption procedure occur at a pH level of 6, accompanied by an adsorbent dosage of 0.02 g for a 25 mL solution volume. Conversely, it is also significant to highlight that a rise in adsorption capacity is detected at a pH of 6. A pH of 6 was originate to be the ideal adsorption pH because of the positive charge of Cd(II) and the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane's zero point of charge (pHzpc) of 4.59. The findings indicate that conducting adsorption at a pH above 4.59 yields significant benefits [36]. The relationship between the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane's adsorption capacity for Cd(II) and its dosage and duration is shown in Fig. 11(b). After 100 min of interaction, the adsorption capacity reaches its maximum effectiveness at a dosage of 0.02 g of the adsorbent, finding a positive correlation with the duration of contact. Fig. 11(c) illustrates the impact of varying contact periods and pH levels on the adsorption capacity of the CS/CQDs/PCL nanofiber membrane for Cd(II) ions. At higher pH values, especially in alkaline environments, the adsorption capacity first rose from 2 to 6 before declining. Additionally, an increase in contact time was associated with an enhancement in adsorption capacity [32,82].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Model validation and the desirability method
ce:section.ce:para: The presented desirability plot systematically outlines the optimized parameters for a specific process by considering various influencing factors. The optimal settings identified include a Dose (A) of 0.02, a Time (B) of 100, and a pH (C) of 6, each achieving a high desirability score of 0.985. This score signifies that these parameters facilitate significantly preferable outcomes. Moreover, the adsorption capacity (qe) is maximized at 323.025, which corresponds to the same desirability score of 0.985, which implies that this value represents the peak performance as well. Ultimately, the consistent desirability score of 0.985 underscores the efficiency with which these parameters contribute to attaining the intended results of the process Fig. 12(a) [75,83]. The desirability plot analyzes optimization involving Dose (A), Time (B), and pH (C). For Dose (A), peak desirability occurs at 0.02, with lower doses preferred as desirability declines with higher doses. Time (B) shows a parabolic relationship, peaking at 100 units, indicating preference for longer durations, though benefits decrease after a certain point. Optimum performance for pH (C) is at around pH 6, with deviations leading to reduced desirability. The plot also shows that adsorption capacity (qe) increases as parameters approach these optimal values, reaching around 300 mg/g. Additionally, qe remains relatively high across various conditions, demonstrating process resilience. Overall, the plot clarifies the best conditions for enhancing adsorption capacity and optimizing process performance, promoting system efficiency Fig. 12(b). The combined desirability plot presented in this image provides a comprehensive assessment of the various factors contributing to the optimization process. The parameters A: Dose, B: Time, and C: pH exhibit desirability values nearing 1.0 (specifically 0.999998, 0.999999, and 0.999999, respectively), signifying that these conditions are nearly fully optimized. Conversely, the qe (adsorption capacity) shows a desirability of 0.940405, indicating that while its performance is slightly less ideal, it remains within a favorable range. The aggregated desirability score of 0.984755 illustrates the overall effective functioning of the system when all factors are taken into account. This score implies that the established process conditions are properly aligned to attain optimal results across the variables examined. Ultimately, the combined desirability score underscores the efficiency of the selected conditions in enhancing the process's overall effectiveness Fig. 12(c) [35].

The analysis of current findings suggests that subsequent research endeavors should prioritize the optimization of electrospinning parameters. This aim is to achieve a more homogenous fiber morphology, which is vital for enhancing the mechanical integrity of the membrane when subjected to industrial conditions. Additionally, it is crucial for further investigations to assess the membrane's long-term stability and regeneration efficiency over numerous operational cycles, thereby evaluating its practical applicability within actual wastewater treatment facilities. Moreover, the process of scaling up the production of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through the utilization of cost-effective and environmentally benign precursors could significantly bolster the economic feasibility of the proposed system. Exploring the membrane's adsorption efficiency in wastewater contaminated with multiple metals, could further broaden its range of applications. Lastly, conducting pilot-scale trials in industrial wastewater treatment plants constitutes an essential next phase. Such trials would serve to confirm the membrane's efficacy under realistic environmental conditions, ensuring compliance with the World Health Organization's recommended limit for cadmium in wastewater, which stands at 0.003 ppm.

This research details the creation of a novel electrospun nanofibrous membrane made up of chitosan (CS), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and polycaprolactone (PCL) aimed at effectively eliminating Cd(II) ions from water. A thorough synthesis and characterization approach was employed, utilizing FT-IR, SEM, XRD, and nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms, which validated the structural and functional characteristics of the nanofibrous membrane. The study systematically analyzed adsorption performance, applying the Box-Behnken Design (BBD) to pinpoint key impelling variables such as pH, Cd(II) concentration, adsorption time, and temperature. The optimal parameters identified for achieving the highest Cd(II) ion removal were a pH level of 6, an adsorbent weight of 0.02 g, and an adsorption time of 100 min, resulting in a remarkable maximum adsorption capability of 341.8 mg/g. The thermodynamic evaluation indicated that the adsorption procedure demonstrates endothermic characteristics, with elevated temperatures resulting in improved adsorption effectiveness. Both pH levels and the initial concentration of Cd(II) were shown to have a significant influence on the adsorption process, while the interplay between temperature and the duration of exposure was determined to be less influential. Additionally, the membrane displayed remarkable reusability, maintaining its adsorption capability across five successive adsorption-desorption cycles with negligible loss in efficiency. The consistency of XRD data collected prior to and following reuse further suggested that the membrane retained its chemical stability and structural integrity, thereby underscoring its viability for practical applications.
